entries.title: Prefer computed() over effect() for derived state
entries.summary: Reach for effect() only for side effects — derived values belong in computed(), which stays pull-based and glitch-free.

paragraphs: A recurring smell in signal code is using effect() to copy one signal into another. It works, but it makes the value push-based and eagerly recomputed, and it runs outside the reactive graph the template consumes.
computed() is the right tool: it is lazy, memoized, and only recalculates when a dependency it actually read has changed.

body.heading: When effect IS right
bullets: Writing to localStorage or the URL when state changes.
Imperatively calling a non-signal API (chart redraw, focus).
Logging / analytics on a state transition.

entries.title: Always give @for a stable track expression
entries.summary: A bad track key re-creates the whole list on every change — wrecking animations, focus and performance.

paragraphs: The new @for block requires a track expression. Track by a stable identity (an id), not by the object reference or $index unless the list is truly static.

entries.title: A tiny debounced signal helper
entries.summary: Wrap any signal in a debounced read — handy for search inputs without pulling in RxJS.

paragraphs: Drop this into a shared util and debounce a source signal without a Subject in sight.

entries.title: Explaining the event loop in an interview
entries.summary: Microtasks vs macrotasks, and why a Promise callback always beats a setTimeout(0).

paragraphs: The call stack runs synchronous code. When it empties, the loop drains the microtask queue (Promises, queueMicrotask, MutationObserver) completely, then takes exactly one macrotask (timers, I/O, events) and repeats.
That is why a resolved Promise callback runs before a setTimeout(0) queued at the same time.
bullets: Macrotasks: setTimeout, setInterval, DOM events, message channel.
Rendering happens between macrotasks, after microtasks drain.

entries.title: The facade pattern for heavy third-party embeds
entries.summary: Show a cheap placeholder and only mount the expensive widget on interaction — the same idea behind lite-youtube.

paragraphs: A facade renders a lightweight stand-in (a poster image and a play button) and defers the real, heavy component until the user actually asks for it. It keeps hundreds of kilobytes of embed off the initial load.
I use exactly this in the portfolio's video player: until you click, there is no iframe in the DOM at all.

entries.title: The layers of caching in a frontend system
entries.summary: From HTTP cache headers to service workers to in-memory signals — where each layer belongs.

paragraphs: Caching is not one decision but a stack of them, each with a different invalidation story.
bullets: CDN / edge: immutable, content-hashed assets, cache forever.
HTTP cache: ETags and max-age for API responses that tolerate staleness.
Service worker: offline shell and stale-while-revalidate for data.
In-memory (signals/resource): dedupe within a session, cleared on reload.

entries.title: Case study: feature-first architecture in this portfolio
entries.summary: How lazy routes, a typed DataService and a shared component barrel keep 30+ pages maintainable.

paragraphs: Every page is a lazy-loaded standalone component under features/. Content lives in JSON, fetched through a single typed DataService built on httpResource, so pages never hand-roll HTTP or hold loading booleans.
Shared UI is exported from one barrel and styled entirely with CSS custom-property design tokens, so a page composes primitives without reaching into their internals.
bullets: core/ — models, services, tokens, constants. No UI.
features/ — one folder per route, lazy-loaded.

entries.title: Bug: an [innerHTML] binding silently broke every image on the page
entries.summary: The server DOM has no innerHTML setter — the throw halted the update pass and stripped the srcset off the hero image.

paragraphs: Symptom: after adding an icon component that set SVG geometry via [innerHTML], the prerendered hero image lost its srcset and src — but only in the SSR output.

Root cause: the server-side DOM shim has no innerHTML setter, so the binding threw during prerendering. Because a throw aborts the rest of the change-detection pass for that view, every binding after it — including the hero's srcset — was silently skipped.

body.heading: The fix
paragraphs: Write the geometry to the element after render instead, via afterRenderEffect, which never runs on the server. Prerendering then emits a bare <svg> frame and the client fills it in, so hydration still matches.

entries.title: Week 29, 2026 — E2E stability and rich media
entries.summary: Tamed WebKit hydration flakiness under parallel Playwright runs and shipped a lazy video + before/after media kit.

paragraphs: This week was mostly about reliability. Desktop-WebKit was timing out under parallel Playwright workers because a single dev server starved on hydration; conditional workers plus toPass + networkidle waits fixed it.
Then I built reusable media components — a facade video player and a before/after slider — and started this Knowledge Hub to keep notes like this one in public.
